planting.note: Seed needs light and cold stratification for best germination; set out after frost danger passes.

directions: This tall, old-fashioned English lavender is intensely fragrant and prized for sachets, potpourri, and cut or dried arrangements. Start seed indoors in a light, well-drained mix, as germination is slow and benefits from a few weeks of cold stratification before sowing. Give plants full sun and sharp drainage; lavender resents wet feet and thrives in lean, alkaline soil. Space transplants well for air circulation and shear lightly after bloom to keep plants compact and productive.

planting.note: Surface-sow tiny seed and keep evenly moist until seedlings establish.

directions: English thyme offers a subtle, woodsy flavor that suits a wide range of dishes and is a key component of bouquet garni. Start seed indoors and barely cover, since the seed is fine and needs some light to sprout; transplant to a sunny, well-drained bed after frost. This drought-tolerant perennial forms a low, spreading mat with small leaves and edible flowers that draw pollinators. Harvest sprigs regularly to encourage bushy growth and avoid overwatering, which can rot the crown.

planting.note: Warmth-loving; do not set out until nights stay above 50 F.

directions: This columnar basil grows into an upright herbal tower of deep, glossy emerald leaves, making it as ornamental as it is useful. It delays flowering up to twelve weeks longer than standard types, so plants keep producing tender leaves without the flavor decline that comes with bolting. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant only after the soil has thoroughly warmed. Its compact, non-branching habit suits patio pots and small beds; harvest leaves often and provide steady moisture and full sun.

planting.note: Also called Florence fennel; direct sow for the bulb, avoid transplant shock that triggers bolting.

directions: Florence fennel forms a swollen, anise-flavored bulb that can be eaten raw, sauteed, or added to soups and pasta, while its feathery leaves garnish fish. Direct sow into rich, moist soil since transplanting can stress plants and cause premature bolting. Keep the bed evenly watered and blanch the bulb by mounding soil around its base as it swells. Grow it as a cool-season crop, timing plantings for spring or fall to avoid the summer heat that pushes it to seed.

planting.note: Surface sow; seed needs light to germinate and self-sows readily.

directions: German chamomile bears masses of small, daisy-like flowers whose apple-scented petals make a soothing tea and lend themselves to dried arrangements and crafts. Sow seed on the soil surface and press in lightly, since it needs light to germinate; it establishes easily and often self-sows for future seasons. Grow in full sun with average, well-drained soil and modest water. Harvest the open flower heads regularly to keep the plant blooming and to build up a supply for drying.

planting.note: Seed needs light and benefits from chilling; press onto surface and do not bury.

directions: Also called Japanese basil or beefsteak plant, shiso carries a complex flavor blending cinnamon, mint, and clove with hints of cumin, and this mix offers both green and red-leaved plants. Surface-sow seed indoors as it needs light to sprout, and set out only after the soil has warmed. Heat- and drought-tolerant, it makes a handsome bedding plant that attracts pollinators. Pinch growing tips to encourage branching and harvest young leaves for wraps, pickles, and garnishes.

planting.note: Set out only after nights stay reliably above 50 F.

directions: Genovese is the classic Italian basil, offering the traditional fragrance and flavor that make it the top choice for pesto and countless summer dishes. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant after the soil is warm and settled weather has arrived, as cold stunts young plants. Give it full sun, rich soil, and even moisture for lush growth. Pinch out flower buds and harvest from the top to keep plants leafy, tender, and productive all season.

planting.note: Warmth-loving; transplant after soil warms.

directions: This exceptionally productive sweet basil produces huge, light green leaves nearly the size of your hand that are more sweet than spicy. The broad leaves make an excellent lettuce substitute in wraps and sandwiches or a bold layer in a caprese salad. Start seed indoors in warmth and move to a sunny bed only after frost danger has passed. Keep soil evenly moist, pinch off flower spikes, and harvest whole leaves to encourage continued production.

planting.note: Direct sow in succession; cilantro dislikes transplanting and bolts in heat.

directions: Also known as Chinese parsley, this variety is selected for excellent flavor, extra leafiness, and slow bolting, giving you a longer cilantro harvest. Direct sow into cool soil since the taproot resents transplanting, and make successive sowings every few weeks for a steady supply. Provide full sun in cool weather or light afternoon shade as temperatures climb to delay flowering. Let some plants bolt and set seed to harvest coriander for the spice rack.

planting.note: Direct sow where it is to grow; the long taproot resents transplanting.

directions: Mammoth dill lives up to its name, reaching at least three feet and sometimes towering to five, with feathery foliage prized for cooking and pickling. Its large flower umbels are magnets for beneficial pollinators, and the plants readily self-sow once seed matures. Direct sow into full sun since the taproot dislikes being moved, and sow in succession for a continuous supply of leaves. Provide staking or a sheltered spot for the tallest plants, and harvest foliage before flowering for the best flavor.

planting.note: Surface sow; seed needs light. Grown as an annual in cold climates.

directions: A member of the marigold family, Mexican tarragon produces anise-flavored leaves that make an easy substitute for French tarragon in warm, humid regions. It handles heat and drought far better than true tarragon and rewards growers with cheerful golden flowers late in the season. Surface-sow seed indoors, as it needs light to germinate, and transplant to full sun after frost. Because the leaves are potent, use them sparingly, and harvest regularly to keep the plant bushy.

planting.note: Direct sow; grown mainly for its abundant coriander seed.

directions: Moroccan coriander is a fast-growing, four-in-one herb, giving a quick flush of leaves, edible white flowers for beneficial insects, and, above all, large aromatic seeds. It is especially valued for its heavy yield of round coriander seed used in baking and spice blends. Direct sow into cool soil since the taproot resists transplanting, and let plants flower and set seed for harvest. Provide full sun and even moisture, then cut seed heads once they turn tan and dry them fully before storing.

planting.note: Slow to germinate; soaking seed overnight speeds sprouting.

directions: This curled parsley forms dense, ruffled rosettes of deep green that garnish plates beautifully and pack in vitamins A, B, and C. Cold-tolerant, it can be grown as an annual that carries on through fall and into mild winters. Parsley seed is famously slow to sprout, so soak it overnight and be patient, keeping the soil consistently moist. Grow in full sun to part shade with steady water and harvest outer stems first to keep the plant producing.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed for a few weeks before sowing to improve germination.

directions: Introduced in 1916 and named for Gertrude Jekyll's garden at Munstead Woods, this compact English lavender blooms earlier than most and stays neatly mounded. Its sweet flowers are among the best for culinary use as well as sachets and dried bouquets. Cold-stratify seed before sowing, then start indoors and transplant to full sun with sharp drainage. Keep water low once established, trim after flowering to maintain a tidy shape, and avoid rich or soggy soil.

planting.note: Transplant only after soil warms and nights stay above 50 F.

directions: Noga basil balances heavy yields with classic Genovese flavor, producing tall, uniform plants that grow fast and stand up to repeated cutting. Its large, glossy, dark green leaves perform well in the field, greenhouse, or a patio container. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant only after the soil has warmed thoroughly. Provide full sun, steady moisture, and frequent harvests, pinching out flower buds to keep leaf production high.

planting.note: Warmth-loving; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Purple Petra offers a mild, sweet basil flavor without the peppery bite of some varieties, making it a favorite for those who prefer gentle herbal notes. Its deep purple foliage doubles as an ornamental accent and tints vinegars and oils a lovely pink. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant after frost, giving plants full sun and rich, moist soil. Pinch tips regularly to encourage bushy growth and keep the vivid leaf color coming.

planting.note: Cover seed well, as calendula germinates best in darkness.

directions: Resina calendula was bred for exceptionally resin-rich petals, making it the go-to variety for herbalists preparing salves and infused oils. Its bright yellow, daisy-like blooms shine in the garden and are so frost tolerant they may keep flowering into the snow. Direct sow and cover the seed well, since darkness aids germination, in a sunny spot with average soil. Deadhead spent blooms to prolong flowering and harvest fully open flower heads on a dry morning for the best medicinal quality.

planting.note: A blend of basil types; all are warmth-loving and set out after frost.

directions: This blend gathers six basils, including Genovese and large-leaf types for pesto, Dark Opal for colorful vinegars, plus lime and lemon basils for oils, sauces, and sorbets. Because every type in the mix is a form of Ocimum basilicum, they share the same warm-weather culture and can be grown together for a diverse harvest. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant after frost into full sun with rich, moist soil. Pinch flower buds and harvest often to keep the range of scents and flavors at their peak.

planting.note: Surface sow the tiny seed, which needs light to germinate.

directions: Sweet Annie, also called sweet wormwood, is beloved by crafters for its lacy, fragrant foliage that fills dried bouquets and wreaths. It grows rapidly into a tall, airy plant, tolerates drought, and gives off a pleasant camphor-like scent that naturally repels moths. Surface-sow the fine seed indoors since it needs light to germinate, then transplant to full sun. Give the tall plants room to spread, cut stems for drying just as they reach full size, and expect self-sown seedlings the following year.

planting.note: Warmth-loving; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Thai basil is a cornerstone of Southeast Asian cooking, prized as the aromatic garnish served alongside Vietnamese pho and stirred into curries and stir-fries. Its anise-scented leaves hold their flavor and texture better through cooking than sweet basils, and its purple stems and flower spikes add ornamental appeal. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant only after the soil warms. Grow in full sun with even moisture, pinching flower spikes to prolong tender leaf production.

planting.note: Direct sow; the taproot resents transplanting.

directions: Tetra is a bushy, late-flowering dill bred for heavy leaf production, so it stays leafy longer before running to seed. Its heat tolerance makes it slow to bolt, letting you pick fresh, aromatic foliage through the warmer months. Direct sow in full sun where plants will grow, since the taproot dislikes being disturbed, and sow in succession for a steady supply. Harvest leaves before flowering for peak flavor, and leave a few plants to bloom for pollinators and seed.

planting.note: Surface sow tiny seed, which needs light to germinate.

directions: True Greek oregano is the genuine culinary type prized by Italian and Greek cooks for its intense, pungent aroma and flavor. It elevates tomato sauces, marinated vegetables, meats, fish, and poultry, and grows compactly enough for containers. Surface-sow the fine seed indoors since it needs light, then transplant to full sun with lean, well-drained soil. Keep water on the low side to concentrate the essential oils, and shear plants after flowering to maintain dense, flavorful growth.

planting.note: First-year flowering perennial; start indoors for earliest bloom.

directions: True hyssop is an easy-care perennial that flowers in its first year and lights up the late-summer garden with spikes that draw bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects. This ancient herb has long been valued for culinary and medicinal use as well as its ornamental blue blooms. Start seed indoors and transplant to full sun with well-drained soil, where it tolerates dry conditions once established. Trim after flowering to keep plants tidy, and grow it near vegetables to attract pollinators.

planting.note: Surface sow; seed needs light and warmth to germinate.

directions: Known as the Queen of Herbs in India, tulsi or holy basil is treasured for its sacred status and prized for tea and wellness uses. This kapoor or temperate type is fast-growing with a distinctive bubble-gum fragrance and better cold tolerance than most basils. Surface-sow seed indoors in warmth since it needs light to germinate, then transplant to full sun after frost. Pinch flower spikes to prolong leaf harvest, though many growers let it bloom to attract pollinators and gather leaves and flowers for tea.

planting.note: Usually grown from a live plant or cutting; set out after frost.

directions: Tuscan Blue is a robust, upright rosemary that grows into an aromatic evergreen shrub prized for both cooking and Mediterranean-style landscaping. Its stiff, fragrant stems are ideal for skewers and roasting, while blue flowers draw pollinators. Because rosemary is slow and erratic from seed, it is best established from a live plant or cutting set out after frost. Give it full sun and fast-draining soil, water sparingly once established, and shelter or overwinter indoors in cold-winter zones.

planting.note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; move outdoors after frost.

directions: The Australian finger lime bears elongated fruit packed with juicy, caviar-like vesicles that burst with tart citrus flavor, making it a chef's favorite. Native to rugged coastal Australia, this compact, thorny citrus is tough and adapts well to container culture. Grow from a potted tree in full sun with rich, well-drained soil, and protect it from frost by moving it indoors in cold climates. Feed with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season and water steadily without letting roots sit wet.

planting.note: Plant the dormant or potted dwarf tree in spring; grown from a grafted tree, not seed.

directions: Bonanza is a genetic dwarf peach that fits easily on a patio yet produces full-size, sweet, rosy fruit. Its naturally compact habit and showy pink spring blossoms make it as ornamental as it is productive, ideal for containers and small yards. Plant a grafted tree in full sun with rich, well-drained soil, and provide a large pot or a spot with room for the roots. Thin the young fruit for better size, prune in late winter to shape, and water consistently while fruit is developing.

planting.note: Plant the dwarf ornamental peach in spring; grown from a grafted tree.

directions: Bonfire is a compact ornamental peach grown chiefly for its striking deep-crimson foliage and pink spring flowers, though it can set small edible fruit. Its fiery color and dwarf size make it a dramatic focal point for patios and small gardens. Plant a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil in a large container or bed. Prune after flowering to maintain its tidy form, water regularly during the growing season, and expect the best leaf color in full sun.

planting.note: Grown from a grafted or air-layered tree; move outdoors after frost.

directions: The Brewster lychee is a classic favorite bearing sweet, juicy fruit encased in a glossy red shell over fragrant white flesh. This subtropical evergreen prefers hot, humid summers and needs a brief cool period to trigger flowering. Grow a grafted or air-layered tree in full sun with acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, and shelter it fully from frost. Keep the root zone consistently moist, mulch heavily, and protect young trees from wind while they establish.

planting.note: Grown from a grafted tree; move outdoors after frost.

directions: The Cara Cara is a sweet, low-acid navel orange distinguished by its rosy-pink flesh and berry-like flavor. This adaptable citrus performs well in the ground in mild regions or in a large container that can be moved indoors where winters are cold. Grow a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil and feed it a citrus-specific fertilizer through the growing season. Water deeply but allow the top of the soil to dry between waterings, and protect it from frost.

planting.note: Grown from a rooted cutting; a climbing cactus needing sturdy support.

directions: Edgar's Baby is a compact dragon fruit, a climbing epiphytic cactus that rewards growers with sweet, vividly colored fruit on a manageable plant. It produces large, fragrant night-blooming flowers followed by fruit and thrives with warmth and strong support to climb. Grow from a rooted cutting in full sun and fast-draining, sandy soil, watering only when the mix dries since the cactus rots in soggy conditions. Provide a trellis or post, feed lightly, and shelter it from any frost.

planting.note: Grown from a grafted tree; move outdoors after frost.

directions: The Eureka is California's go-to commercial lemon, a strong grower that produces tart, juicy fruit nearly year-round. It bears reliably and adapts well to container culture, making it a practical choice for both mild-climate yards and patios that overwinter indoors. Grow a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil and feed with a citrus fertilizer during active growth. Water deeply and let the surface dry between waterings, and shield the tree from frost.

planting.note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; plant after frost.

directions: Frantoio is a renowned Italian olive prized for producing high-quality oil and rich, dark fruit that ripens mid-season. This evergreen brings old-world Mediterranean character to the garden and tolerates heat and drought once established. Grow a young tree in full sun with lean, well-drained soil, watering sparingly since olives dislike wet roots. Prune to an open framework for light and air, and in marginal climates grow it in a container to shelter from hard freezes.

planting.note: Grown from a grafted tree; move outdoors after frost.

directions: Key lime, also called Mexican lime, produces small, intensely aromatic and juicy fruit beloved for pie, drinks, and marinades. This tender, somewhat thorny citrus stays compact and grows readily in a container that can shelter indoors in cold weather. Grow a grafted tree in full sun with excellent drainage and feed it a citrus fertilizer during the growing season. Water deeply, letting the top of the soil dry between waterings, and protect it from any frost.

planting.note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; plant after frost.

directions: Koroneiki is a productive Greek olive celebrated for generous harvests and a compact, easygoing habit that suits both orchards and patios. Its small fruit yields a robust, high-quality oil, and the silvery evergreen foliage lends year-round Mediterranean charm. Grow a young tree in full sun with sharp drainage and minimal water once established. Prune to keep light reaching the interior, and where winters are cold, grow it in a pot to move to shelter during freezes.

planting.note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; plant after frost.

directions: The Mission olive, first cultivated in California, is a versatile favorite valued for both flavorful oil and classic table olives. It is a hardy, resilient evergreen that adapts to a range of conditions and delivers reliable harvests for home growers. Plant a young tree in full sun with well-drained soil and water sparingly once it establishes. Prune for an open canopy and good airflow, and in cold-winter regions grow it as a container specimen that can be sheltered from hard freezes.

planting.note: One of the more cold-tolerant citrus; grown from a grafted tree.

directions: The Owari satsuma is the early bird of citrus, ripening juicy, seedless, easy-peel mandarins in early winter. It is among the most cold-tolerant citrus, making it a good choice for growers in warm-temperate regions or in containers that overwinter indoors. Grow a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil and feed with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season. Water deeply but allow the surface to dry between waterings, and give some frost protection during hard cold snaps.

planting.note: Grown from a grafted tree; more frost-tolerant than most avocados.

directions: Pancho is a cold-hardy Mexican-type avocado that shrugs off chills that would damage tender varieties, extending avocado growing into cooler regions. It is low-maintenance and rewards patient growers with creamy, homegrown fruit. Plant a grafted tree in full sun with deep, fast-draining soil, since avocados are extremely sensitive to soggy roots and root rot. Water regularly but never let the tree stand in water, mulch well, and protect young trees during their first few winters.

planting.note: Plant the dormant or potted tree in spring; grown from a grafted tree.

directions: Prairie Fire is an ornamental crabapple that puts on a spectacular show of magenta spring blossoms followed by fiery fall foliage and small persistent fruit that feeds birds. Disease-resistant and adaptable, it makes a durable landscape tree for cold and temperate gardens. Plant a grafted tree in full sun with well-drained soil, watering regularly until established. Prune in late winter to shape and remove crossing branches, and enjoy the tiny fruit as wildlife forage or for jelly.

planting.note: Grown from a grafted or potted tree; move outdoors after frost.

directions: Ruby Supreme is a tropical guava that bears fragrant fruit with sweet, pink-red flesh rich in flavor and nutrition. This fast-growing evergreen fruits young and adapts well to containers, making it accessible even where winters are cold. Grow in full sun with rich, well-drained soil, and feed regularly during the warm season for the heaviest crops. Keep the soil evenly moist, prune to maintain a manageable size, and bring potted trees indoors before frost.

planting.note: Grown from a pup or potted plant, not seed; move outdoors after frost.

directions: Truly Tiny is a miniature Dwarf Cavendish banana that stays compact enough for a container or a bright corner indoors while still producing edible fruit. Its bold tropical leaves make it an ornamental standout, and its small size suits patios and sunrooms. Grow from a potted plant or pup in full sun with rich, moisture-retentive soil, feeding heavily during active growth. Keep the soil consistently moist since bananas are thirsty, shelter it from wind, and move it indoors before frost.

planting.note: Grown from a potted shrub or cutting; plant after frost danger passes.

directions: The Blueberry Pie butterfly bush produces dense clusters of deep blue-violet, sweetly fragrant blooms that draw butterflies, bees, and hummingbirds all summer. This vigorous flowering shrub is easy to grow and provides months of pollinator activity in the sunny border. Plant a potted shrub in full sun with well-drained soil and water moderately until established. Deadhead spent flower spikes to prolong bloom and prune hard in early spring to keep the plant compact and full of new flowering wood.
